summary: The plan has internalized the core DX priorities (zero-config crud, typed errors, Mermaid/trace, scaffolder, npm-test exit criterion, fixHint in error serialization) and the pre-R1 triage already caught the most obvious DX-coherence bugs (the `npm run dev` contradiction, the createdAt determinism gap). Remaining DX gaps fall into three classes: (1) assertion 2 of the exit criterion silently assumes IVM propagation is synchronous before `post:list` returns — a 15-minute-developer would hit this as a flaky test; (2) scaffolder first-install latency and prebuilt-binary strategy are undefined, putting the 10-minute promise at risk on cold cargo machines; (3) debugging ergonomics (TRANSFORM parse errors pointing at user source, Mermaid consumer UX, trace pretty-printing, source-map-style stack attribution) are assumed to 'just work' with no plan commitment. None of this is architecturally hard; all of it is cheap to fix in the plan before R5. Ship the two major items as plan edits, accept the minors as backlog into G8 mini-review.

findings.location: Section 1 assertion 2 + Section 2.3 I2 + Section 5 Rank 2
findings.claim: Exit criterion assertion 2 ('Three creates + list reflects all three') silently assumes the IVM subscriber has flushed View 3 by the time `engine.call('post:list')` returns from create #3. The plan's Rank 2 recommendation is a **bounded tokio broadcast** change-stream with 'on overflow, mark views stale' semantics — which is inherently asynchronous. If the subscriber runs on a separate task, the test is a race: 3 creates, then list — does the evaluator block until the subscriber has applied the three ChangeEvents, or does list read a stale view? A developer on day one should NEVER encounter a flaky CI test on the scaffolder template. A developer on day one should ALSO NOT have to learn the phrase 'IVM consistency barrier' just to make their tests pass.
findings.evidence: Section 1 assertion 2: 'engine.call(post:create, …) × 3, then engine.call(post:list) returns exactly those three posts'. Section 2.3 I2: 'Change-stream subscriber ... routes each ChangeEvent'. Section 5 Rank 2: 'bounded tokio broadcast ... with a configurable high-water mark that, on overflow, marks affected views as stale'. Nowhere does the plan commit to 'engine.call returns only after IVM has caught up to this tx' semantics, which is the only thing that makes assertion 2 non-flaky.
findings.fix: Add an explicit DX commitment under Section 2.6 N3 (or a new N8): `engine.call` on a handler that produces WRITEs does not return until the IVM subscriber has processed the resulting ChangeEvents through all views the subgraph transitively reads. Two implementation options for R1 debate: (a) synchronous in-process view update inside the same transaction commit (simple but couples IVM to txn path — violates evaluator-is-ignorant-of-IVM); (b) async subscriber + per-tx barrier that `engine.call` awaits before returning. Option (b) preserves the architectural thesis. Either way: document it, test it, and make it the default. A `engine.call_fire_and_forget()` variant can expose the async path for power users. The thinness-test engine (no IVM subscriber at all) naturally has no barrier and `call` returns immediately.
findings.dx_impact: Developer running `npm test` on a freshly scaffolded project sees a 30% flaky rate. They file a bug, don't trust the engine, and leave. Exit criterion must be 100% deterministic.

findings.claim: The plan commits `npm install @benten/engine` + `npx create-benten-app my-app` + `npm install` + `npm test` as the 10-minute path. It does not commit to a prebuilt-binary distribution strategy for the napi-rs crate. If `npm install @benten/engine` triggers a source build because no prebuilt matches the user's platform, the user needs a Rust toolchain installed, and a cold `cargo build` of the workspace is ~2–5 minutes on a modern Mac — half the 10-minute budget. On unsupported architectures (e.g. Linux arm64 musl, Windows x86_64) the install fails outright with a Rust-compiler error message the target developer is not equipped to diagnose.
findings.evidence: Section 2.7: bindings crate has no mention of `@napi-rs/cli` prebuild publishing, no mention of `prebuildify` / `napi-rs` per-platform binary targets (`@benten/engine-linux-x64-gnu`, `@benten/engine-darwin-arm64`, etc.). Section 2.8 T1: scaffolder shipped without any statement on which platform binaries are guaranteed. Section 5 has no 'install-latency' or 'prebuild-distribution' risk. QUICKSTART.md line 9 promises 'npx create-benten-app my-app / npm install / npm run dev' as the headline path.
findings.fix: Add deliverable B8 to Section 2.7: '`napi-rs` prebuilt binary matrix for {darwin-arm64, darwin-x64, linux-x64-gnu, linux-arm64-gnu, win32-x64-msvc}. CI publishes prebuilt binaries on every release tag to the npm registry as per-platform optional deps. Source build is the fallback path and must print a clear banner on entry ('Building Benten from source — this takes ~3 minutes. For instant install, use a supported platform: …')'. Add Rank 11 to Section 5: 'Install-latency prebuild coverage — the 10-minute DX promise depends on prebuilts for the 5 common platforms. Phase 1 must ship them; what's the CI publish pipeline?'. Add a scaffolder-test CI leg that measures cold `npm install` wall-clock and fails the build if it exceeds 60 seconds on the reference runner.
findings.dx_impact: A developer on an unsupported platform hits a Rust compiler error as their first-ever Benten interaction. A developer on a supported platform who happens to hit a prebuild gap waits 5 minutes before seeing any Benten code run. Either outcome breaks the 10-minute promise.

findings.claim: TRANSFORM is a user-authored expression language (Section 2.5 E4, Section 5 Rank 4 recommends hand-rolled Pratt parser). Structural invariant 12 (registration-time validation) means TRANSFORM parse errors should fire at `engine.registerSubgraph(sg)` — not at first call. But the plan does not specify parse-error ergonomics. A developer writing `transform({ total: '$input.price * input.qty' })` (missing `$`) gets what message, pointing at what location, with what fix hint? If the answer is 'E_INV_TRANSFORM_PARSE_ERROR: parse failed at character 18' without quoting the source line or identifying which Node's TRANSFORM expression it was, the developer is hunting blind through a subgraph they described in TS but error-reported in Rust-speak. ERROR-CATALOG.md has codes for every structural invariant but zero codes in the E_TRANSFORM_* range.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E4: 'TRANSFORM expression evaluator ... Subset of JavaScript ... arithmetic, comparison, logical, ternary, property access, array methods ...'. Section 2.5 E5: 'All invariant failures map to E_INV_* codes' — but TRANSFORM parse is not an invariant. ERROR-CATALOG.md lines 21-90 cover E_INV_* for structural invariants; no E_TRANSFORM_PARSE, E_TRANSFORM_UNKNOWN_BUILTIN, E_TRANSFORM_UNDEFINED_BINDING. Section 5 Rank 4 recommends 'hand-roll a Pratt parser' + 'publish the BNF as docs/TRANSFORM-GRAMMAR.md' but doesn't speak to error ergonomics.
findings.fix: Add to Section 2.5 E4: 'TRANSFORM expression parse errors are reported at register-time per invariant 12. Every parse failure produces an E_TRANSFORM_* code with (a) the Node CID containing the expression, (b) the expression string, (c) 1-indexed character position, (d) a caret-underlined snippet, (e) a fix hint. The DSL wrapper layer (B6) maps the Node CID back to the TS source position by matching against the `subgraph(…)` call-site metadata captured when the DSL compiled.' Add to ERROR-CATALOG.md: E_TRANSFORM_PARSE (unexpected token), E_TRANSFORM_UNKNOWN_BUILTIN (builtin not in whitelist), E_TRANSFORM_UNDEFINED_BINDING (reference to $result before a step produced it), E_TRANSFORM_FORBIDDEN (closure / prototype access / this). Fix hints must be concrete: 'Did you mean `$input.qty`?' not 'invalid expression'. Add G6-B deliverable: `transform_parse_error_has_source_position`, `transform_parse_error_identifies_node_cid`, `transform_parse_error_suggests_likely_typo`.
findings.dx_impact: A developer's first non-trivial handler (anything beyond `crud('post')`) will involve TRANSFORM. If TRANSFORM parse errors are cryptic, the developer bounces on their second serious session, not their first — but just as hard.

findings.claim: When `engine.call('post:create', …)` throws at runtime (e.g. a WRITE hits `E_WRITE_CONFLICT`, or a BRANCH has no matching case, or a TRANSFORM hits division by zero at run time), what stack trace does the developer see? Node.js convention is a JS stack pointing at their `engine.call` site plus a JS frame per internal caller. But the actual error originates inside the Rust evaluator walking Nodes that the developer authored as `write(…).onConflict(…)` three screens back. The plan commits to `engine.trace()` as the debugging surface (T3), but that requires the developer to (a) know the feature exists, (b) know to wrap their failing call in a trace, (c) interpret the TraceStep array. Default error ergonomics — what you see when you just `await engine.call(…)` in a node REPL and it throws — matter more than power-user ergonomics. A thrown error must answer: which Node, authored by which DSL call, on which TS source line, did this come from?
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E3: 'typed error edges (ON_NOT_FOUND, ON_EMPTY, ON_CONFLICT, ON_DENIED, ON_ERROR, ON_LIMIT)'. Section 2.8 T3: 'engine.trace(handler, input) surfaced through DSL'. Section 2.7 B7: 'typed error classes (ECapDenied, EInvCycle, …)' — the shape per ERROR-CATALOG.md is `{code, message, context, fixHint}`. Nowhere in the plan is `context.node_cid → TS source location` mapping specified. Nowhere is there a DX commitment along the lines of 'error.stack includes DSL call-sites'.
findings.fix: Add deliverable B9 to Section 2.7: 'DSL source-map. When B6 compiles a `subgraph(…).read(…).write(…)` chain into Nodes, it captures `new Error().stack` at each DSL-function call-site and stores the parsed (file, line, col) into a per-subgraph sourcemap indexed by Node CID. When the napi layer throws, it attaches the sourcemap lookup to `error.stack` under a `dslLocation` field and prepends a synthetic frame `at <dsl_call> (<file>:<line>:<col>)`. This is what lets a developer read an error thrown from `engine.call` and jump to the exact DSL line they wrote.' Add to Section 4.5: `tests/thrown_error_contains_dsl_source_location`. The `diag` feature flag (default on via benten-engine) is the right home for the sourcemap machinery; production deployments can strip it.
findings.dx_impact: The first time a handler throws in anger, the developer needs to know WHERE in their code. Without this, they'll either (a) always wrap calls in `engine.trace()` pre-emptively (expensive, ugly) or (b) leave. Power-user ergonomics via `engine.trace()` are fine; default ergonomics are the make-or-break.

findings.location: Section 2.5 E7 + Section 2.8 T2 + QUICKSTART.md line 81
findings.claim: `handler.toMermaid()` returns a string. QUICKSTART says 'Prints a Mermaid diagram you can paste into any Markdown viewer'. The plan does not commit to (a) a recommended viewer, (b) an `engine.openMermaid()` or equivalent that spawns the default browser pointing at mermaid.live with the diagram pre-loaded, (c) an `engine.toMermaidUrl()` that returns a mermaid.live share link, or (d) a docs-site page explaining the one-line copy-paste path. The DX gap is narrow but the handoff from 'I called toMermaid and got a string' to 'I can see my subgraph' is undocumented friction.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E7: `subgraph.toMermaid() — serialize any operation subgraph as a Mermaid flowchart`. Section 2.8 T2: `subgraph.toMermaid() surfaced through DSL`. QUICKSTART.md line 81: `console.log(postHandlers.create.toMermaid())`.
findings.fix: Add two helper methods in B6: (a) `handler.toMermaidUrl()` returns `https://mermaid.live/edit#pako:…` with the diagram already embedded (pako-compress + base64 the Mermaid source per mermaid.live's format); (b) document in README and QUICKSTART that `console.log(handler.toMermaidUrl())` produces a shareable link. Bonus: recommend the VSCode 'Markdown Preview Mermaid Support' extension in CONTRIBUTING.md. Costs ~30 LOC in B6; saves every developer 60 seconds on their first Mermaid encounter.
findings.dx_impact: Developer encounters this on their first 'let me see what my handler looks like' attempt. Friction is 60 seconds, not 10 minutes — but it's friction where the payoff was supposed to be 'wow, I can see my handler'.

findings.claim: `engine.trace(handler, input)` returns `Vec<TraceStep { node_cid, inputs, outputs, duration_us, error }>`. In the smoke test (exit criterion assertion 4) this shape is asserted mechanically. But in interactive usage — a developer pastes this into `console.log()` — they get a multi-line Object dump with raw CID strings and no visual hierarchy. For a 10-Node handler the output is ~80 lines of barely-readable JS object dump. Pretty-printing matters.
findings.evidence: Section 2.5 E8: 'Produces Vec<TraceStep { node_cid, inputs, outputs, duration_us, error }>'. Section 2.8 T3: 'Returns {steps: TraceStep[], result}'. No pretty-print helper, no `trace.print()`, no `trace.toString()` override.
findings.fix: In B6, attach a `[util.inspect.custom]` (Node) and `toString()` to the trace object that renders a tabular step-by-step view: `Node | Type | Input → Output | Duration | Error?` with ANSI color for errors, muted durations under 10µs, bold for the failing step. Also expose `trace.toMermaid()` that renders the traced path highlighted against the full subgraph. ~100 LOC in the wrapper.
findings.dx_impact: Developer encounters on first debug session. Ugly trace output makes them think 'is this really the debug surface?'. Good pretty-print turns the same surface into a wow moment.

findings.claim: The scaffolder template contents are under-specified. Section 2.8 T1 says 'minimal TS project with @benten/engine installed, a sample handler file, and npm run dev / npm run test wired'. But there's no mention of: (a) a 'NEXT STEPS' banner printed at the end of `npx create-benten-app` pointing the developer to the 3 most useful docs; (b) a second handler file that shows a non-crud subgraph (so the developer has a working non-trivial example to modify); (c) a `.github/workflows/ci.yml` for the user's project; (d) a README.md in the scaffolded project with links to Benten docs. The scaffolder is the developer's FIRST interaction — its template quality is disproportionately load-bearing.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 T1 content is one sentence. No enumeration of template files.
findings.fix: Add to Section 2.8 T1 deliverable: 'The scaffolder template ships: (a) `src/handlers/post.ts` with `crud(post)` zero-config, (b) `src/handlers/publish.ts` with a non-trivial subgraph (read post, branch on status, write publish event, respond) so the developer has a working non-crud example; (c) `README.md` in the project with a "What you just got" section linking to VISION.md / QUICKSTART.md / DSL-SPECIFICATION.md on the Benten docs site; (d) `test/smoke.test.ts` (the 6-assertion exit-criterion test); (e) a final-screen banner printed by the scaffolder: "✓ Done! cd my-app && npm test to verify. Next: open src/handlers/post.ts and try changing the schema."' Costs one day of template authoring; sets the tone of the entire developer relationship.
findings.dx_impact: Developer sees the scaffolded project as 'their' project — its quality signals Benten's quality. A bare-bones template says 'figure it out'. A curated template says 'we thought about you'.

findings.claim: The plan commits `createdAt` injection on the zero-config `crud('post')` path. But DSL-SPECIFICATION.md §4 (CrudOptions) references `timestamps: boolean` that adds both `createdAt` AND `updatedAt`. The plan does not commit to `updatedAt` — does zero-config `crud('post')` also inject `updatedAt` on update? What about `id` (is it the CID? a uuid?)? What about `authorId` (derived from caller context)? Without a decision, the scaffolder's `crud('post')` path produces posts with `createdAt` but without `updatedAt`, which breaks any developer expectation that CRUD includes the standard audit fields.
findings.evidence: Section 2.7 B6: 'Zero-config crud(post) must inject createdAt: <deterministic HLC timestamp> on WRITE if the input does not include one'. DSL-SPECIFICATION.md §4.1 CrudOptions: 'Add timestamps (createdAt, updatedAt) automatically. Default: true.' No mention in the plan of `updatedAt`, `id`, or author attribution.
findings.fix: Extend Section 2.7 B6 to: 'Zero-config crud(X) injects (a) `createdAt: <HLC>` on create if absent, (b) `updatedAt: <HLC>` on create and on every update, (c) `id: <CID string>` (since CIDs are already content-addressed IDs, this is the natural choice; developers who want uuids supply their own), (d) no `authorId` in Phase 1 — author attribution is a Phase 2 capability since capability enforcement hardening across all primitives is Phase 2.' Add test `crud_zero_config_injects_complete_audit_fields` to §4.5.
findings.dx_impact: Developer on first crud call, inspecting the returned post, notices `createdAt` but no `updatedAt`, assumes partial implementation. Not a dealbreaker — but a 'this feels unfinished' signal.

findings.claim: B6 commits to 'good TS types' but does not specify discriminated unions, template literal types, or inference that makes DSL misuse a compile-time error. E.g. `read('post', { id: … })` should auto-complete property names from `contentType:post` schema if one is registered; `branch([{ when: '...' }, ...])` should error at compile time if any branch is missing a RESPOND terminator; `call('notifications/send-email', ...)` should error if 'notifications/send-email' isn't a registered handler id. The DSL-SPECIFICATION.md §3 type shapes use string-typed `target`, `expression`, and `subgraphId` — compile-time-string-checked with no inference. The DX difference between 'the DSL has types' and 'the DSL's types catch real bugs' is large.
findings.evidence: Section 2.7 B6: 'good TS types'. DSL-SPECIFICATION.md §3.2 SubgraphBuilder: `read(target: string, options?: ReadOptions): ReadChain` — `string` everywhere. No generics on content-type label, no phantom types on terminal-vs-non-terminal steps, no template-literal type for capability strings.
findings.fix: Add to Section 2.7 B6 the following type-system commitments: (a) `crud<T extends string>(label: T): CrudHandlers<T>` with generics carrying the label through the return type so `ctx.call('post:create', input)` auto-completes the valid handler ids; (b) template literal type on capability strings: `'store:post:write' | 'store:post:read' | …`; (c) a phantom `Terminal` brand on `RespondStep` so `subgraph(…).write(…).compile()` without a `respond()` is a compile-time error; (d) inference from registered schemas if the developer supplies `schema: z.object({…})` (zod) — the DSL resolves field types. This is a 2-3 day effort in G8-B; the ROI is 'half the DSL bugs never reach runtime'.
findings.dx_impact: TypeScript developers expect tight types. Loose types across the DSL's surface signal 'this is really a Rust library with a TS escape hatch', not 'this is a TS-first library'. Affects perception more than first-day productivity.

findings.location: Section 2.1 C7 + Section 2.7 B7 + Section 2.8 T7
findings.claim: Section 2.1 C7 says napi layer serializes errors as `{code, message, context, fixHint}`. T7 codegens TS error types from the catalog. But the plan does not confirm: (a) is `fixHint` always populated (every error in the catalog), (b) is it exposed on the thrown JS error as `error.fixHint` (accessible), (c) is it ALSO printed by default in `error.toString()` / `error.message` so `console.error(err)` surfaces it without the developer knowing to look for `.fixHint`? ERROR-CATALOG.md includes fix hints on every error but the serialization path is ambiguous.
findings.evidence: Section 2.1 C7: 'serialized in napi layer as {code, message, context, fixHint}'. Section 2.7 B7: 'typed error classes (ECapDenied, EInvCycle, …) generated from docs/ERROR-CATALOG.md'. ERROR-CATALOG.md entries consistently have Fix fields. No commitment that `err.toString()` renders the fix hint.
findings.fix: Extend Section 2.7 B7 deliverable: 'Every typed error class overrides `toString()` / `[Symbol.toPrimitive]` to render: `<CODE>: <message>\n  fix: <fixHint>\n  context: <context JSON>`. The fixHint surfaces on `console.error(err)` without requiring the developer to know the field exists. Add test `error_tostring_includes_fix_hint` to §4.5.
findings.dx_impact: First error a developer sees: if the default `console.error` output includes a fix hint, developer self-serves. If the hint is hidden behind `.fixHint` field access, they don't know it exists and ask in Discord instead. Cheap win.

findings.claim: Phase 1 ships a DSL (B6), a scaffolder (T1), typed errors (B7), Mermaid output (E7), tracing (E8) — and zero documentation deliverable. The plan has no committed doc site, no TSDoc build step, no README updates, no 'getting-started' page beyond QUICKSTART.md (which is marked 'specification of intent'). On ship day, the engine exists but anyone learning it has to read CLAUDE.md + 16 docs in docs/. That's unreasonable for an external developer.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 deliverables T1-T9: no doc-site build. Section 4 (test landscape): no docs tests. QUICKSTART.md closing lines 97-106: 'If you're reading this pre-Phase-1, treat it as a specification of intent rather than a working tutorial.' Post-Phase-1 the docs need to be a working tutorial.
findings.fix: Add deliverable T10 to Section 2.8: 'Minimal README.md overhaul in the repo root: installation, 30-second example, link to QUICKSTART.md (which is updated post-Phase-1 to be a working tutorial), link to DSL-SPECIFICATION.md, link to ERROR-CATALOG.md. TSDoc comments on every exported function in `@benten/engine`. No docs site required for Phase 1 — but QUICKSTART.md MUST be updated to remove the "specification of intent" banner and replaced with working copy-pasteable commands. Add a CI check that greps the current QUICKSTART.md for the phrase "not yet built" and fails if found.' This costs half a day and prevents the worst first-day experience (landing on a README that's out of date).
findings.dx_impact: Developer lands on GitHub README, reads 'Phase 1 complete — ship crate 0.1.0', clicks through to QUICKSTART.md, sees 'treat it as specification of intent', and leaves confused. The blocker is 30 minutes of doc work.

findings.claim: The 10-minute DX path is gated by cold `npm install` + first `cargo build` (if prebuild miss) + first `npm test`. If the scaffolder test template imports ONLY the prebuilt napi binding, `npm test` is fast (<10s cold). If any part of the scaffolded project template triggers a Rust source rebuild, the 10-minute clock is blown. The plan doesn't specify.
findings.evidence: Section 2.8 T1: 'npm run test wired'. No commitment on whether the template pulls `@benten/engine` as pure npm dep (prebuilt binary) or includes any dev-only Rust build steps.
findings.fix: Add to Section 2.8 T1: 'Scaffolder template MUST depend only on published `@benten/engine` (prebuilt binaries per platform). The template's `npm install` must not trigger any Rust compilation under any platform where a prebuild is published. CI test: `scaffolder_cold_install_under_60s` runs `npm install` in a fresh container and asserts wall-clock <60s on every supported platform.' Ties this finding together with finding on prebuild coverage.
findings.dx_impact: The 10-minute promise is the marketing claim. It has to hold on a cold machine, not just in the committer's warm-cargo-cache environment.

merge_notes: Two of my major findings (exit-criterion-ivm-synchronicity, install-latency-prebuilds) overlap in spirit with architect-reviewer and code-reviewer territory — the IVM-synchronicity finding is arguably an architecture concern I framed as DX; the prebuilds finding is arguably a build-engineering concern I framed as DX. Merge by claim+location, not by agent-lens.
The transform-parse-error-ergonomics and call-stack-attribution findings are DX-specific and should not be expected from other R1 agents.
The crud-defaults-completeness finding may overlap with benten-engine-philosophy on scope discipline (is authorId in Phase 1?).
None of my findings contradict the pre-R1 triage's fixes. The triage's B6 `createdAt` injection is correct; I'm extending it to full audit-field coverage.
Every finding has a concrete fix; none require deferring to Phase 2 or re-architecting. The plan is DX-salvageable with the 4 major fixes in Section 2 + ERROR-CATALOG extensions.
